texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Constant-product market making functions were first introduced by Hayden Adams in 2017 to create Uniswap, a decentralised exchange on Ethereum. This enables users to exchange assets at any given rate. Some variations such as Balancer and Curve were later introduced. In this paper, we analyse the maths that rule this type of protocol. We show that splitting a trade in multiple smaller trades does not impact the final exchange rate. We also show that the protocol is safer and more profitable when no one recompounds their fees.
texts.text: Constant-product market making functions were first introduced by Hayden Adams in 2017 to create Uniswap, a decentralised exchange on Ethereum. This enables users to exchange assets at any given rate. Some variations such as Balancer and Curve were later introduced. In this paper, we analyse the maths that rule this type of protocol. We show that splitting a trade in multiple smaller trades does not impact the final exchange rate. We also show that the protocol is safer and more profitable when no one recompounds their fees.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In October 2008, Satoshi Nakamoto published the Bitcoin whitepaper [8]: the first cryptocurrency , a decentralised currency that no one can control. Since Satoshi Nakomoto's revolutionnary paper, a lot of effort has been made to try decentralising things. In 2014, Vitalik Buterin published the whitepaper [3] of his own blockchain: Ethereum the first one implementing smart contracts , a piece of code that is run on the blockchain. This technological breakthrough enabled the making of the first Decentralised Finance (DeFi) protocol MakerDao [4]. The latter enabled users to make collateralized debt position using cryptocurrencies, ie. deposit cryptocurrency to borrow money. Since then, the number of DeFi protocols skyrocketted and the total value locked in DeFi smart contracts reached a $250b all time high [1] in december 2021. In this paper, we focus on one specific type of DeFi protocol: decentralised exchanges. The first decentralised exchange protocol, Uniswap [2], was proposed by Hayden Adams in 2017 and was released in 2018 on the Ethereum blockchain. Uniswap is an automated market maker (AMM): users can lock cryptocurrencies on a smart contract, which will be used as liquidity to market make. Uniswap uses a constant product market making model, which we discuss later. This model works well in general but is not optimal for token pairs which exchange rate is stable. For this purpose, Michael Egorov created another protocol, Curve [5], that uses another maket making model that focuses on stable token pairs.
texts.text: In October 2008, Satoshi Nakamoto published the Bitcoin whitepaper [8]: the first cryptocurrency , a decentralised currency that no one can control. Since Satoshi Nakomoto's revolutionnary paper, a lot of effort has been made to try decentralising things. In 2014, Vitalik Buterin published the whitepaper [3] of his own blockchain: Ethereum the first one implementing smart contracts , a piece of code that is run on the blockchain. This technological breakthrough enabled the making of the first Decentralised Finance (DeFi) protocol MakerDao [4]. The latter enabled users to make collateralized debt position using cryptocurrencies, ie. deposit cryptocurrency to borrow money. Since then, the number of DeFi protocols skyrocketted and the total value locked in DeFi smart contracts reached a $250b all time high [1] in december 2021. In this paper, we focus on one specific type of DeFi protocol: decentralised exchanges. The first decentralised exchange protocol, Uniswap [2], was proposed by Hayden Adams in 2017 and was released in 2018 on the Ethereum blockchain. Uniswap is an automated market maker (AMM): users can lock cryptocurrencies on a smart contract, which will be used as liquidity to market make. Uniswap uses a constant product market making model, which we discuss later. This model works well in general but is not optimal for token pairs which exchange rate is stable. For this purpose, Michael Egorov created another protocol, Curve [5], that uses another maket making model that focuses on stable token pairs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The betanet version of Beaker uses a Uniswap v2 [6] model with two changes: fees are not automatically compounded in the pool and users can trade with a given spread. In this paper, we break down the maths behind this protocol and explore various questions that can arise when interacting with it.
texts.text: The betanet version of Beaker uses a Uniswap v2 [6] model with two changes: fees are not automatically compounded in the pool and users can trade with a given spread. In this paper, we break down the maths behind this protocol and explore various questions that can arise when interacting with it.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Maths for a constant-product AMM
texts.text: 2 Maths for a constant-product AMM

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we explore the maths behind the fixed product model. In this model, pools containing two assets are set up and any individual (called a liquidty provider) can provide liquidity to it. These pools act as markets makers and provide liquidity at any given rate. More elaborate models enable each individual to provide liquidity for a given price range [7].
texts.text: In this section, we explore the maths behind the fixed product model. In this model, pools containing two assets are set up and any individual (called a liquidty provider) can provide liquidity to it. These pools act as markets makers and provide liquidity at any given rate. More elaborate models enable each individual to provide liquidity for a given price range [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the rest of the paper we consider a pool made of tokens X and Y . Let x (resp. y ) be the amount of tokens X (resp. Y ) in the pool, and let r be the exchange rate for the pool.
texts.text: In the rest of the paper we consider a pool made of tokens X and Y . Let x (resp. y ) be the amount of tokens X (resp. Y ) in the pool, and let r be the exchange rate for the pool.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start by choosing the definition of liquidity and the exchange rate of our pool. These define how our pool is going to market make.
texts.text: We start by choosing the definition of liquidity and the exchange rate of our pool. These define how our pool is going to market make.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are two equations that are at the center of the model. The first one states that the product of the number of tokens should be a constant and is often called fi xed product equation. In mathematical terms, this means that the geometric mean should always stay constant. This geometric mean is what we call liquidity .
texts.text: There are two equations that are at the center of the model. The first one states that the product of the number of tokens should be a constant and is often called fi xed product equation. In mathematical terms, this means that the geometric mean should always stay constant. This geometric mean is what we call liquidity .

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, the fixed product assumption just means that the liquidity is constant during a trade.
texts.text: Therefore, the fixed product assumption just means that the liquidity is constant during a trade.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Axiom 2.1. L is an invariant of a trade.
texts.text: Axiom 2.1. L is an invariant of a trade.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Then, we must decide how our pool is going to make the market ie. how it is going to choose the exchange rate. A natural idea is to choose r = -∂x ∂y . In fact, r is the number of tokens X we receive when we add an infinitesimal amount of tokens Y to the pool. For simplicity, we use the result of the previous computation as the definition of r .
texts.text: Then, we must decide how our pool is going to make the market ie. how it is going to choose the exchange rate. A natural idea is to choose r = -∂x ∂y . In fact, r is the number of tokens X we receive when we add an infinitesimal amount of tokens Y to the pool. For simplicity, we use the result of the previous computation as the definition of r .

texts.orig: Now that we have defined these concepts, we can define what is the value of the pool.
texts.text: Now that we have defined these concepts, we can define what is the value of the pool.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Axiom 2.2 (Arbitrage assumption) . We assume that the exchange rate of the pool is always equal to the exchange rate of the market:
texts.text: Axiom 2.2 (Arbitrage assumption) . We assume that the exchange rate of the pool is always equal to the exchange rate of the market:

texts.label: formula

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In reality, when the exchange rate of the pool is not equal to the one of the market, arbitrageurs can make a small profit by restoring the equality. Therefore, this assumption holds in practice.
texts.text: In reality, when the exchange rate of the pool is not equal to the one of the market, arbitrageurs can make a small profit by restoring the equality. Therefore, this assumption holds in practice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the next subsections, we will give various relations between the variables of the pool and we will describe how these variables change when a trade is done. We later explore the notion of Impermanent Loss , which characterizes the risks of a liquidity provider and we conclude by evaluating the evolution of the portfolio of liquidity providers.
texts.text: In the next subsections, we will give various relations between the variables of the pool and we will describe how these variables change when a trade is done. We later explore the notion of Impermanent Loss , which characterizes the risks of a liquidity provider and we conclude by evaluating the evolution of the portfolio of liquidity providers.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3 Impermanent Loss
texts.text: 2.3 Impermanent Loss

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Something that is often overlooked is the impermanent loss that the providers of the pool can face. It represents the risk that a provider takes by providing liquidity. Therefore the fee f of the pool not only rewards the providers for the service they are offering but also the risk they are willing to take. But what is impermanent loss? Impermanent loss is the relative difference between the value of a portfolio invested in a pool and the value it would have if it had not been invested. It is called impermanent because this loss depends on the current rate of exchange of both tokens X and Y . Hence, the loss can fluctuate and is only realised when providers remove liquidity from the pool. In this section, x and y will represent the initial number of tokens held by the user and x ′ and y ′ will represent these same numbers after being invested in the pool and submited to some fluctuations of the exchange rate r .
texts.text: Something that is often overlooked is the impermanent loss that the providers of the pool can face. It represents the risk that a provider takes by providing liquidity. Therefore the fee f of the pool not only rewards the providers for the service they are offering but also the risk they are willing to take. But what is impermanent loss? Impermanent loss is the relative difference between the value of a portfolio invested in a pool and the value it would have if it had not been invested. It is called impermanent because this loss depends on the current rate of exchange of both tokens X and Y . Hence, the loss can fluctuate and is only realised when providers remove liquidity from the pool. In this section, x and y will represent the initial number of tokens held by the user and x ′ and y ′ will represent these same numbers after being invested in the pool and submited to some fluctuations of the exchange rate r .

texts.text: In Figure (1) we plot Λ when only one of the tokens' price changes. To have a better picture of what is happening we also plot in Figure (2) the relative portfolio comparison.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These plots are misleading because they give the impression that one is always better off not providing liquidity. This is because the accrued fees are not taken into account in the impermanent loss.
texts.text: These plots are misleading because they give the impression that one is always better off not providing liquidity. This is because the accrued fees are not taken into account in the impermanent loss.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4 Relative portfolio evolution with time
texts.text: 2.4 Relative portfolio evolution with time

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As seen in the previous section, impermanent loss does not take into account accrued fees and can therefore be misleading. Before providing liquidity, it is important to understand the winning scenarii and the risks that one is willing to take. In this section, we explore the evolution of a porfolio when providing liquidity or not and according to the way the fees are dealt with. There are 2 ways of dealing with trading fees :
texts.text: As seen in the previous section, impermanent loss does not take into account accrued fees and can therefore be misleading. Before providing liquidity, it is important to understand the winning scenarii and the risks that one is willing to take. In this section, we explore the evolution of a porfolio when providing liquidity or not and according to the way the fees are dealt with. There are 2 ways of dealing with trading fees :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Automatically reinject the fees as liquidity (e.g Uniswap v2);
texts.text: Automatically reinject the fees as liquidity (e.g Uniswap v2);

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Collect the fees and store them separately (e.g Beaker);
texts.text: Collect the fees and store them separately (e.g Beaker);

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the rest of the section we respectively reference them as fee model (1) and (2).
texts.text: In the rest of the section we respectively reference them as fee model (1) and (2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As swaps occure unpredictably, we consider the evolution according to a given liquidity growth α (% per year) and we assume, for simplicity, that the growth is linear (ie. that the swaps happen as the same frequence with the same value).
texts.text: As swaps occure unpredictably, we consider the evolution according to a given liquidity growth α (% per year) and we assume, for simplicity, that the growth is linear (ie. that the swaps happen as the same frequence with the same value).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2.7 (Relative evolution for fee model (1)) . Let δ x and δ y be the relative price change of X and Y and t the time passed in years. Then, in the fee model (1), the relative evolution ∆ 1 ( t ) = V p ( t ) V ( t ) of the portfolio of a liquidity provider is:
texts.text: Theorem 2.7 (Relative evolution for fee model (1)) . Let δ x and δ y be the relative price change of X and Y and t the time passed in years. Then, in the fee model (1), the relative evolution ∆ 1 ( t ) = V p ( t ) V ( t ) of the portfolio of a liquidity provider is:

texts.text: Proof. We first compute the number x ( t ) of tokens X that should be in the pool if the price does not change. When compounding with a liquidity evolution α , the liquidity of the pool will grow by α per year. Therefore, we have:

texts.orig: When the rate of the pool does not change, both tokens grow equally, therefore:
texts.text: When the rate of the pool does not change, both tokens grow equally, therefore:

texts.text: Because, L does not depend on r , we can reuse x ′ = √ δ y δ x x and the rest of the proof for the impermanent loss. It gives:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: V p ( t ) = 2 p x x √ δ y δ x (1 + αt ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dividing by the initial value of the portfolio V = 2 xp x ends the proof.
texts.text: Dividing by the initial value of the portfolio V = 2 xp x ends the proof.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark. One could think that with automatic compounding, the value of the portfolio should grow exponentially. In fact, it is only true if we assume that the the exchange volume of the pool grows at the same rate as the liquidity of the pool. However, there is no reason that this should be true. Some exchanges implicitly make this false assumption when they compute the expected APY of the pool.
texts.text: Remark. One could think that with automatic compounding, the value of the portfolio should grow exponentially. In fact, it is only true if we assume that the the exchange volume of the pool grows at the same rate as the liquidity of the pool. However, there is no reason that this should be true. Some exchanges implicitly make this false assumption when they compute the expected APY of the pool.

texts.orig: Theorem 2.8 (Relative evolution for fee model (2)) . Let δ x and δ y be the relative price change of X and Y and t the time passed in years. Then, in the fee model (2), the relative evolution ∆ 2 ( t ) = V ′ ( t ) V ( t ) of the portfolio of a liquidity provider is:
texts.text: Theorem 2.8 (Relative evolution for fee model (2)) . Let δ x and δ y be the relative price change of X and Y and t the time passed in years. Then, in the fee model (2), the relative evolution ∆ 2 ( t ) = V ′ ( t ) V ( t ) of the portfolio of a liquidity provider is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∆ 2 ( t ) = √ δ y δ x + αt δ x + δ y 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. In the fee model (2) the accrued fees are stored separately and not compounded. With our assumption on fees accumulation, the accumulated amount of tokens x out ( t ) in the separate storage is such that :
texts.text: Proof. In the fee model (2) the accrued fees are stored separately and not compounded. With our assumption on fees accumulation, the accumulated amount of tokens x out ( t ) in the separate storage is such that :

texts.text: Therefore the final value of the portfolio is:

texts.text: We therefore get :

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure (3) compares the two fee model for a liquidity growth α = 20%. This figure show that fee model (2) is both safer and more profitable than fee model (1). Intuitively, rewards, when compounded, are also prone to impermanent loss. However, there is a catch: what happens in fee model (2) when some people regularly compound and others don't?
texts.text: Figure (3) compares the two fee model for a liquidity growth α = 20%. This figure show that fee model (2) is both safer and more profitable than fee model (1). Intuitively, rewards, when compounded, are also prone to impermanent loss. However, there is a catch: what happens in fee model (2) when some people regularly compound and others don't?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5 Return on Investment
texts.text: 2.5 Return on Investment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At the end of the previous section, we asked an important question. To answer it, we are first going to compute the return on investment for users compounding and users not compounding their fees. We start by computing the liquidity evolution for people compounding their rewards.
texts.text: At the end of the previous section, we asked an important question. To answer it, we are first going to compute the return on investment for users compounding and users not compounding their fees. We start by computing the liquidity evolution for people compounding their rewards.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2.9. Let L c (resp. L nc ) be the liquidity of the pool owned by people compounding (resp. not compounding) their rewards and L 0 be the initial liquidity of the pool. Then, L c satisfies the differential equation:
texts.text: Theorem 2.9. Let L c (resp. L nc ) be the liquidity of the pool owned by people compounding (resp. not compounding) their rewards and L 0 be the initial liquidity of the pool. Then, L c satisfies the differential equation:

texts.text: Definition 2.6 (Return on investment for compounding users) . For compounding users, we call return on investment the ratio

texts.text: Definition 2.7 (Return on investment for not compounding users) . For not compounding users, we call return on investment the ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because the solution of the previous differential equation has no simple expression, an equation solver was used. A worst case scenario (99% users compounding) is plotted in Figure (4). The plot shows that users compounding have a 20 . 02% ROI and the others a 18 . 20% ROI instead of the initial 20% expected ROI.
texts.text: Because the solution of the previous differential equation has no simple expression, an equation solver was used. A worst case scenario (99% users compounding) is plotted in Figure (4). The plot shows that users compounding have a 20 . 02% ROI and the others a 18 . 20% ROI instead of the initial 20% expected ROI.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark. Because users only lose 1 . 8% ROI by not recompounding it is therefore likely that they would not care.
texts.text: Remark. Because users only lose 1 . 8% ROI by not recompounding it is therefore likely that they would not care.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From this, it is now possible to compare the portfolio evolution of users that recompound their fees and those who don't. This comparison is made in Figure (5). This new comparison shows that users that recompound have a slight hedge when the relative price does not change too much. Still, as pointed out in previous remark, regularly compounding rewards for a little premium does not seem worth it. Therefore, users investing in Beaker pools should expect their portfolio to change as described in (3).
texts.text: From this, it is now possible to compare the portfolio evolution of users that recompound their fees and those who don't. This comparison is made in Figure (5). This new comparison shows that users that recompound have a slight hedge when the relative price does not change too much. Still, as pointed out in previous remark, regularly compounding rewards for a little premium does not seem worth it. Therefore, users investing in Beaker pools should expect their portfolio to change as described in (3).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Conclusion
texts.text: 3 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To sum up, our analysis has shown that investing in a liquidity pool using a constant-product market making function boils down to making a bet on the price evolution of both tokens. We have also proven that the protocol is safer and more profitable when no one recompounds fees.
texts.text: To sum up, our analysis has shown that investing in a liquidity pool using a constant-product market making function boils down to making a bet on the price evolution of both tokens. We have also proven that the protocol is safer and more profitable when no one recompounds fees.
